metadata.description: Quantified market patterns derived from peer-reviewed academic research. Each pattern includes source, historical return premium, confidence level, activation conditions, and practical notes.
metadata.confidence_scale: HIGH=robust post-publication, multi-study, >$1B capacity | MODERATE=exists but conditions-dependent | LOW=weakened/arbitraged

patterns.name: Cross-Sectional Price Momentum (12-1 Month)

academic_source.primary: Jegadeesh & Titman (1993), Journal of Finance 48(1), pp. 65-91
academic_source.secondary: Jegadeesh & Titman (2001), NBER Working Paper w7159
academic_source.supporting: Carhart (1997) 4-factor model; Asness, Moskowitz & Pedersen (2013) AQR

signal_construction.universe: Any liquid equity universe; originally NYSE/AMEX
signal_construction.long_portfolio: Top decile or top 20-30% by trailing return
signal_construction.short_portfolio: Bottom decile or bottom 20-30% by trailing return

historical_return_premium.long_only_annual: 2-4% excess return over market on long side

patterns.status: ACTIVE — persistent post-publication; one of most replicated anomalies in finance

works_poorly_in: Bear market rebounds (crash risk — losses of 30-91% documented)
Choppy sideways markets with frequent reversals
conditions.regime_filter: Apply only when broad market price > 200-day moving average
conditions.volatility_filter: Reduce position size when VIX > 25; go flat when VIX > 35

crash_risk.mitigation: Dynamic sizing based on predicted mean and variance; 200-day MA filter

patterns.notes: Skip 1 month is critical — avoids short-term reversal effect. Time-series momentum (Moskowitz 2012) provides independent confirmation signal. Factor momentum (momentum of factor returns) is a newer variant with similar properties.

patterns.name: Time-Series Momentum (Absolute Momentum)

academic_source.primary: Moskowitz, Ooi & Pedersen (2012), Journal of Financial Economics 104(2)
academic_source.supporting: AQR Research; Gary Antonacci 'Dual Momentum Investing' (2014)

signal_construction.signal: positive if asset return over past 12 months is positive; negative if negative
signal_construction.asset_classes: Works across 58 futures: equity indices, bonds, commodities, currencies

historical_return_premium.raw_annual: ~11-14% annualized across asset classes (1985-2012 original sample)
historical_return_premium.note: Independent of cross-sectional momentum; both share positive auto-covariance source

conditions.works_best_in: Trending macro environments; works across multiple asset classes
conditions.best_use: Equity index ETFs, sector ETFs — go long when index has positive 12-month return

patterns.notes: Simpler implementation than cross-sectional: just check if asset is up or down over past 12 months. Works across equities, bonds, commodities, FX. Provides portfolio-level trend signal.

patterns.name: Size Premium (SMB — Small Minus Big)

academic_source.primary: Fama & French (1993), Journal of Financial Economics 33, pp. 3-56
academic_source.supporting: Banz (1981) original documentation; Fama-French data library (Ken French website)

patterns.confidence: LOW (standalone) — MODERATE (combined with momentum or value)
patterns.status: WEAKENED — Raw premium largely arbitraged in U.S. large/mid-caps; survives in micro-cap and international

works_best_in: Micro-cap universe (<$300M market cap) — but high transaction costs
Combined with value screen (cheap small caps)
works_poorly_in: Risk-off environments (small caps sell off harder in bear markets)
High yield spread environments (credit tightening hits small caps more)

patterns.notes: January seasonality for small caps has residual effect in first 5 trading days of January. Do not use SMB as standalone signal in modern U.S. markets. Best as a tilt within a multi-factor framework.

patterns.name: Value Premium (HML — High Minus Low Book-to-Market)

academic_source.primary: Fama & French (1993), Journal of Financial Economics 33, pp. 3-56
academic_source.secondary: De Bondt & Thaler (1985) Journal of Finance 40(3) — behavioral explanation
academic_source.supporting: Asness, Moskowitz & Pedersen (2013) — value across asset classes

signal_construction.quality_overlay: Add profitability filter to avoid value traps (RMW factor)

historical_return_premium.2010_2020_period: Near zero to negative (growth massively outperformed)
historical_return_premium.post_2022: Value recovered significantly with rate hikes

patterns.status: ACTIVE but regime-dependent — requires patience; underperforms in low-rate, growth-dominant regimes

works_best_in: Rising interest rate environments (hurts long-duration growth stocks more)
works_poorly_in: When combined with deteriorating fundamentals (avoid value traps)

patterns.notes: Book value increasingly less meaningful due to intangibles revolution. Better value metrics: EV/EBITDA, P/FCF, price-to-sales adjusted for margins. Combine always with quality (profitability) screen to avoid buying fundamentally broken companies.

patterns.name: Low Volatility / Low Beta Anomaly

academic_source.primary: Black, Jensen & Scholes (1972); Haugen & Heins (1975) — original documentation
academic_source.modern: Baker, Bradley & Wurgler (2011) Journal of Portfolio Management
academic_source.supporting: Frazzini & Pedersen (2014) 'Betting Against Beta' — AQR; robust in 20+ countries

signal_construction.short_portfolio: Top quintile by trailing beta/volatility (or simply avoid high-beta)

historical_return_premium.raw_return: Low-vol stocks earn similar or higher raw returns than high-vol
historical_return_premium.persistence: Robust; one of most stable anomalies across time and geography

patterns.status: ACTIVE — persistent due to structural reasons (benchmark constraints, leverage limits)

works_best_in: Bear markets and high-volatility environments (defensive characteristics shine)
Long-only portfolios where Sharpe improvement matters
works_poorly_in: Strong momentum bull markets (high-beta stocks outperform)

patterns.behavioral_explanation: Investors overpay for 'lottery ticket' high-vol stocks. Benchmark-relative mandates push institutional managers toward high-beta. Leverage constraints prevent rational arbitrage. These structural forces are unlikely to disappear.
patterns.notes: Best implemented via minimum-variance portfolio construction. Also accessible via low-vol ETFs (SPLV, USMV). Combine with dividend yield for income + defensive portfolio.

patterns.name: January Effect — Small Cap Seasonality

academic_source.primary: Rozeff & Kinney (1976); Keim (1983) Journal of Financial Economics

signal_construction.timing: First 5 trading days of January (concentrated effect)
signal_construction.universe: Small-cap stocks; recent 12-month losers (tax-loss selling candidates)
signal_construction.mechanism: Tax-loss harvesting in December → rebound buying in January

historical_return_premium.original_magnitude_small_cap: 3-6% excess return in January vs. other months
historical_return_premium.modern_magnitude: Near-zero for large caps; 1-2% residual for micro-caps
historical_return_premium.trend: Steadily declining since 1980s as awareness increased

patterns.status: MOSTLY DEAD in large/mid-caps — weak residual in micro-caps and international markets with different tax regimes

conditions.works_in: Micro-caps in taxable accounts where year-end selling pressure is high
conditions.does_not_work_in: Large caps, ETFs, tax-advantaged account universe
conditions.secondary_effects: November-December may show selling pressure from tax-loss harvesting setup

patterns.notes: Halloween Effect (Sell in May) is a related but more persistent seasonal anomaly: November-April avg +7%, May-October avg +2% historically. Use as minor allocation tilt, not primary signal.

patterns.name: Post-Earnings Announcement Drift (PEAD)

academic_source.primary: Bernard & Thomas (1989) Journal of Accounting Research; Bernard & Thomas (1990) Journal of Accounting and Economics
academic_source.modern: Philadelphia Fed Working Paper 21-07 (2021) — text-based PEAD enhancement

signal_construction.metric: Standardized Unexpected Earnings (SUE) = (Actual EPS - Consensus EPS) / Std Dev of past forecast errors
signal_construction.simple_alternative: EPS beat or miss percentage vs. consensus
signal_construction.additional_signal: Management guidance direction: raised = stronger drift; cut = stronger negative drift

historical_return_premium.modern_post_cost: 8-15% annualized in mid-cap universe after transaction costs

patterns.status: ACTIVE — most persistent anomaly in accounting/finance literature; reduced but not eliminated

works_best_in: Low analyst coverage names (slower information absorption)
When guidance is raised (confirms the beat is not one-time)
When analyst revisions follow in same direction post-announcement
works_poorly_in: Large-cap S&P 500 names (fast institutional reaction absorbs drift quickly)
When market-wide volatility is very high (beta swamps alpha)
conditions.entry_timing: 2-5 days after announcement (let initial volatility settle; bid-ask narrows)
conditions.exit_timing: Day before next earnings announcement; or at 60 trading day mark

patterns.notes: Guidance matters more than EPS beat. A 5% EPS beat with guidance maintained is weaker than a 3% beat with raised guidance. Text sentiment from earnings call transcripts provides additional alpha beyond numeric surprise (2021 Fed study). Analyst estimate revision tracking for 30 days post-announcement strengthens signal.

patterns.name: VIX Mean-Reversion / Volatility Risk Premium

academic_source.primary: Engle (1982) ARCH — Econometrica 50(4); Bollerslev (1986) GARCH — Journal of Econometrics 31(3)
academic_source.modern: Carr & Wu (2009) Volatility Risk Premium study; Liu, Ning & Zhang (2018) VRP analysis
academic_source.applied: CBOE VIX methodology (2003); Realized GARCH model (Hansen & Huang 2016, J. Financial Econometrics)

volatility_risk_premium.definition: VIX (implied vol) minus subsequent realized 30-day vol
volatility_risk_premium.strategy: Sell options to collect this premium; requires tail-risk management

historical_return_premium.vix_spike_3m: VIX > 40: subsequent 3-month return > +10% in most historical cases
historical_return_premium.vrp_strategy: Short-vol systematic strategies: Sharpe ~1.0-1.3 with periodic severe drawdowns

patterns.status: ACTIVE — VIX mean-reversion is one of the most reliable tactical signals in markets

conditions.signal_type: Contrarian — spike in VIX is an opportunity, not a warning to stay out
conditions.works_best: Spot equity long positions after extreme VIX spikes (>40)
conditions.risk: VIX can stay elevated for months in structural bear markets (2008-2009); need stop-loss
conditions.vrp_risk: Short-vol strategies can lose 50%+ in crash events (Feb 2018, March 2020)

vix_term_structure.contango: Normal — front month VIX < back month; indicates low near-term stress; favorable for short-vol
vix_term_structure.backwardation: Stress — front month VIX > back month; acute fear; potential buy signal for equities
vix_term_structure.signal_use: Steep backwardation = extreme fear = contrarian buy; steep contango = complacency = risk-off warning

patterns.notes: More than 65% of U.S. equities show statistically significant mean-reverting volatility. GARCH(1,1) is robust baseline; Realized GARCH significantly outperforms conventional GARCH for VIX forecasting. The volatility risk premium is persistent and has theoretical grounding in risk aversion and demand for portfolio insurance.

patterns.name: Shiller CAPE Ratio — Long-Term Valuation Signal

academic_source.primary: Shiller, R.J. (1981) 'Do Stock Prices Move Too Much?' American Economic Review
academic_source.measurement: Campbell & Shiller (1988) 'The Dividend-Price Ratio and Expectations of Future Dividends and Discount Factors' Review of Financial Studies
academic_source.predictive_power: Goyal & Welch (2008); Faber (2012) 'Global Value' — international CAPE application

signal_construction.formula: CAPE = Current Price / (10-year average of real, inflation-adjusted EPS)
signal_construction.purpose: Smooths out cyclical earnings fluctuations to get normalized valuation

historical_return_premium.timing_r_squared: <5% for 1-year return prediction (essentially useless for short-term)

patterns.status: ACTIVE as long-term asset allocation signal; UNRELIABLE as market timing tool

valid_uses: Comparing U.S. vs. international valuation (relative CAPE)
Setting return expectations for retirement planning
Determining size of equity allocation in multi-asset portfolio
invalid_uses: Predicting bear markets or market peaks (cannot)
Short-selling equities based on high CAPE alone
Any tactical decision with <3 year horizon
conditions.structural_caveat: Tech/platform company dominance in S&P 500 may justify structurally higher CAPE vs. historical average; intangibles not in book value; buybacks reduce share count increasing EPS

patterns.notes: CAPE remained above 25 from 1996-2000 and markets gained 100%+ before the crash. High CAPE = poor expected future returns, not imminent crash. Use as valuation guardrail and allocation weight, not as binary in/out signal.

patterns.name: Profitability Factor (RMW — Robust Minus Weak)

academic_source.primary: Fama & French (2015) Journal of Financial Economics 116(1), pp. 1-22
academic_source.supporting: Novy-Marx (2013) 'The Other Side of Value' — gross profitability predicts returns

signal_construction.metric: Operating profitability = (Revenue - COGS - SG&A - Interest) / Book Equity

historical_return_premium.combined_with_value: 4-6% when combined with cheap valuation (profitable value)

patterns.status: ACTIVE — robust addition to factor model; avoids value trap problem
patterns.notes: Critical use: overlay on value screen. Cheap + profitable = strong expected return. Cheap + unprofitable = value trap. RMW screen eliminates most value traps.

patterns.name: Investment Factor (CMA — Conservative Minus Aggressive)

academic_source.primary: Fama & French (2015) Journal of Financial Economics 116(1), pp. 1-22
academic_source.supporting: Cooper, Gulen & Schill (2008) 'Asset Growth and the Cross-Section of Stock Returns'

signal_construction.metric: Annual asset growth rate = (Total Assets(t) - Total Assets(t-1)) / Total Assets(t-1)
signal_construction.long_portfolio: Bottom tertile — conservative investors (low asset growth)
signal_construction.short_portfolio: Top tertile — aggressive investors (high asset growth)

historical_return_premium.mechanism: Aggressive investment often signals management overconfidence or empire-building; returns diluted; shareholders overpay for growth that doesn't materialize

patterns.status: ACTIVE — useful as negative screen; avoid companies with aggressive asset growth without profit improvement
patterns.notes: Primary use: negative screen. Avoid companies growing assets >20%/year without corresponding profit growth. Acquisitive conglomerates often destroy value.
